coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical analysis of heat transfer enhancement inside concentric counter flow tube heat exchanger using different nanofluids
coredata.dc:creator: Salameh, Tareq
Alkasrawi, Malek
Olabi, Abdul Ghani
Makky, Ahmed Al
Abdelkareem, Mohammad Ali
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this paper is to study experimentally and numerically heat transfer enhancement and friction factor inside a counter flow concentric tube heat exchanger using different volume fractions of CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids. Using nanofluids increases the thermal conductivity and conductive heat transfer coefficients compared to the base fluids. Increased heat transfer coefficients reduce the required tube length leading to reduced pressure drop in heat exchangers. Nanofluids have a high specific surface area, and therefore more heat transfer surface between particles and fluids is available. A standard k-ε turbulence model based on two equations was used in this investigation to solve the momentum and energy equations of nanofluids. The results were presented for Reynolds number range between 2000 and 12,000 and two different volume fractions of 0.05 and 0.2%, respectively. The numerical results illustrated that all the tested nanofluids exhibited better heat transfer coefficients compared with pure water. Particularly, CuO nanofluid illustrated the highest performance index for both concentrations. The performance index varied from 4.25, 3, and 2.5 to 1.5 for CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids, respectively, for the whole range of Reynold numbers. The results of this investigation may be used in designing heat exchangers-based nanofluids that can be used in many energy applications such as solar, energy production systems, and any industrial process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluids
Concentric tube heat exchanger
Standard k-ε turbulence model
Thermo- hydraulic performance index

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast nomenclature orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Picture of the experimental setup (b) Schematic diagram of the experimental setup.
(a) Computational domain, (b) description of the mesh type, and (c) boundary conditions used in the concentric tube heat exchange.
Contour plot of temperature for (a) CuO nanofluid (b) Al2O3 nanofluid, and (c) TiO2 nanofluid.
Numerical Nusselt number for different nanofluids (a) 0.05% volume fraction and (b) 0.2% volume fraction.
Comparison of the average Nusselt number between experimental [20,21] and numerical results for different nanofluids (a) 0.05% volume fraction and (b) 0.2% volume fraction.
Numerical results for the average Nusselt number ratio of nanofluid to pure water using different nanofluids at various Reynolds numbers and volume fractions.
Numerical results for the overall heat transfer coefficient ratio of nanofluid to pure water using different nanofluids at various Reynolds numbers and volume fractions.
Numerical results (a) Pressure drop and (b) average friction factor inside the inner tube for pure water and CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids at 0.05% and 0.2% volume fraction.
Effect of varying the Reynolds number and volume fraction on the ratio of the nanofluid friction factor to the pure water.
The effect of varying the Reynolds number and volume fraction of nanofluids on the thermo-hydraulic performance index inside the inner tube.
Physical properties of base fluid (water) and nanofluid in the inner pipe at 0.05% and 0.2% volume fraction (please also show the results for 0.05% for better comparison).

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical analysis of heat transfer enhancement inside concentric counter flow tube heat exchanger using different nanofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sustainable Energy & Power Systems Research Centre, RISE, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, P.O. Box 27272, United Arab Emirates

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sustainable Energy & Power Systems Research Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSustainable Energy & Power Systems Research Centre, RISE, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, P.O. Box 27272, United Arab Emirates

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bIndustrial Assessment Centre, University of Wisconsin Milwaukee, Milwaukee, WI, United States of America

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Design
School of Engineering and Applied Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Mechanical Engineering and Design, School of Engineering and Applied Science, Aston University, Aston Triangle, Birmingham, B4 7ET, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Minia University, Minia, 61111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dChemical Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, Minia University, Minia, 61111, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this paper is to study experimentally and numerically heat transfer enhancement and friction factor inside a counter flow concentric tube heat exchanger using different volume fractions of CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids. Using nanofluids increases the thermal conductivity and conductive heat transfer coefficients compared to the base fluids. Increased heat transfer coefficients reduce the required tube length leading to reduced pressure drop in heat exchangers. Nanofluids have a high specific surface area, and therefore more heat transfer surface between particles and fluids is available. A standard k-ε turbulence model based on two equations was used in this investigation to solve the momentum and energy equations of nanofluids. The results were presented for Reynolds number range between 2000 and 12,000 and two different volume fractions of 0.05 and 0.2%, respectively. The numerical results illustrated that all the tested nanofluids exhibited better heat transfer coefficients compared with pure water. Particularly, CuO nanofluid illustrated the highest performance index for both concentrations. The performance index varied from 4.25, 3, and 2.5 to 1.5 for CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 nanofluids, respectively, for the whole range of Reynold numbers. The results of this investigation may be used in designing heat exchangers-based nanofluids that can be used in many energy applications such as solar, energy production systems, and any industrial process.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Production term of turbulent kinetic energy
Over all heat transfer coefficient [W/ m2. °C]

ce:section.ce:para: Recent progresses in nanotechnology have led to the development of a new type of heat transfer fluids called nanofluids, created by dispersing nanoparticles between 10 and 50 nm in classical heat transfer fluids [1]. The resulting nanofluids exhibited superior thermal characteristics compared with traditional fluids. Many research work related to heat transfer enhancement using nanofluids has been investigated by many researchers both experimentally and theoretically [1–4]. The heat transfer enhancement of nanofluids depends on many parameters such as thermal properties, size and shape of nanoparticles, type and volume fraction, and many others [5].

Convection heat transfer mechanisms are widely used in renewable energy applications for heating solar water collector systems or cooling for solar photovoltaic systems [6]. Many types of research showed that adding nanoparticles to base fluid enhanced the heat transfer characteristics of the thermal systems. For example, the heat transfer enhancement inside heat exchangers was studied experimentally by many authors [7–11]. Rohit et al. [7] and Bherouz et al. [8] studied experimental heat transfer enhancement in concentric tube heat exchangers using Al2O3 nanofluid for various inlet temperatures and volume concentrations. Their results indicated better heat transfer enhancement when adding nanoparticles compared with pure water as a base fluid. Senthilraja and Vijayakumar [9] analyzed experimental heat transfer enhancement inside double pipe heat exchanger using CuO-water nanofluid for various Reynolds number and volume fractions. Their results showed that the heat transfer coefficient increased with an increase in both Reynold number and volume fraction of nanoparticles.

An experimental investigation was carried out by Darzi et al. [10] to study the effect of Al2O3 nanofluid on heat transfer, pressure drop, and thermal performance of a double-tube heat exchanger. Experiments were carried out at different Reynolds numbers ranging from 5000 to 20,000, approximately, and in various nanoparticles concentrations up to 1% by volume. Results indicated a good potential in promoting the thermal performance of heat exchangers by adding nanoparticles in the investigated ranges where there is no severe pressure drop penalty [12]. Albadr et al. [11] studied experimentally forced convective heat transfer characteristics of Al2O3-water nanofluid in a horizontal shell and tube heat exchanger counter flow under turbulent flow conditions and different volume concentrations of nanofluid (03%−2%). Their results showed that the nanofluid's convective heat transfer coefficient was slightly higher than that of the base liquid at the same mass flow rate and inlet temperature.

Further, the heat transfer coefficient of the nanofluid was found to increase with an increase in the mass flow rate and volume concentration of the Al2O3 nanofluid. Reddy and Rao [13] analyzed experimentally the heat transfer coefficient and friction factor of TiO2 nanofluid flowing in a double pipe heat exchanger with and without helical coil inserts. The experiments were conducted in the range of Reynolds numbers from 4000 to 15,000 and in the volume concentration range from 0.0004% to 0.02%. Their results indicated that the heat transfer coefficient and friction factor of ethylene glycol water-based TiO2 nanofluid enhanced by 10.73% and 8.73% for 0.02% volume concentration of nanofluid when compared to base fluid flowing in a tube. The experimental results showed that helical coil inserts further enhanced heat transfer and friction factor by 13.85% and 10.69%, respectively, for 0.02% nanofluid compared to the base fluid.

The literature also considered numerical investigations to study thermal heat transfer characteristics and friction factors in heat exchangers using nanofluids [14–16]. Moreover, the hybrid of nanofluids between CuO and Al2O3 was studied in the U tube heat exchange applications as in Gürbüz et al. [17]. Tiwari et al. [18] conducted a numerical study using Ansys Fluent commercial software to investigate heat transfer and fluid flow in a single pass counter flow chevron corrugated-plates plate heat exchanger considering nanofluids (CeO2 and Al2O3 - water). The results of their numerical simulation were compared with experimental data to verify the accuracy of the homogeneous model. CFD simulation showed that the plate's corrugation pattern develops turbulence and fluid vortices, which results in high heat transfer rates. Akhtari et al. [19] presented an experimental and numerical study on the heat transfer of α-Al2O3/water nanofluid flowing through the double pipe and shell and tube heat exchangers under laminar flow conditions. Effects of important parameters such as hot and cold volume flow rates, nanofluid temperature, and nanoparticles concentration on the heat transfer characteristics were investigated. The results indicated that the heat transfer performance of both double pipe and shell and tube heat exchangers increases with increasing the hot and cold volume flow rates, as well as the particle concentrations and nanofluid inlet temperature.

Moreover, the authors indicated that the heat transfer performance of nanofluid in a shell and tube heat exchanger is 26.2% higher than the double pipe heat exchanger. Hosseinnezhad et al. [20] conducted a numerical study on the turbulent flow of water/Al2O3 nanofluid in a tubular heat exchanger with two twisted-tape inserts using the finite volume method. The investigated parameters of their study were Reynolds numbers in the range of 10,000–30,000, the effect of the twist ratio of twisted-tape inserts from 2.5 to 4, co-swirl flow and counter-swirl flow of two twisted tapes inside the tube, and volume fractions of nanofluid from 1 to 4%. Their results indicated that the average Nussselt number increased with an increase in the volume fraction of Al2O3 nanoparticles and a decrease in the twist ratio. The stability of nanofluids is one of the important things during its work, and many researchers have studied the stability of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in spiral and serpentine mini channels [21–23].

The objective of the present study is to investigate experimentally and numerically the effect of using three different nanofluids (CuO, Al2O3, and TiO2 –water) on the heat transfer and fraction factor using two different volume fractions of nanoparticles (0.05% and 0.2%). Using nanofluids increases the thermal conductivity and conductive heat transfer coefficients compared to the base fluids. Increased heat transfer coefficients reduce the required tube length, reducing pressure drop in heat exchangers. Nanofluids have a high specific surface area, so more heat transfer surface between particles and fluids is available. The finite volume method was used for computational fluid dynamics (CFD) to solve conservation equations, and the turbulence model based on two-equation standard k-ε was used during these simulations to close the problem for turbulent viscosity.
The phases of the methodology consist of the following sections.

The complete setup consists of the concentric tube heat exchanger, and two circulating pumps, each with a flow rate of 6 L/s, attached with a ball valve and a rotameter to the inlet of each tube. Ball valves provided on each tube can be opened and closed to allow the flow of water from the tank through the tubes, while rotameters were connected to measure the flow rate of cold and hot water from the tubes, and two storage tanks for hot and cold fluid. Four K-type thermocouples with ± 0.25 °C attached to the acquisition system were used to measure the inlets and outlet temperatures of the heat exchanger, two for each tube. Immersion-type water heater associated with temperature controller, coating of mineral wool. Finally, the cladding of rubber solar pipe insulation in the tubes was used as thermal insulation in order to reduce heat loss. The temperature at four different positions at the outer surface of the test section was measured by thermocouple altogether in order to get the average Nusselt number. These thermocouples were connected to the data logger sets as follows:
T1- Hot water inlet to the heat exchanger
T2- Hot water outlet from the heat exchanger
T3- Cooling water inlet to the heat exchanger
T4- Cooling water return from the heat exchanger

Miniature thermocouple plugs take these signals to the temperature indicator and data logger; at the end, each temperature and flow rate are going to be measured by the thermocouple and mass flow meter and stored by the data acquisition system. Fig. 1 shows the details of the experimental setup used in this research.

The concentric tube heat exchanger used for studying the performance of three different types of nanofluid is shown in Fig. 1. The structural details of the heat exchanger are 69 cm in length and 1.27 cm and 2.45 cm for inner and outer diameters, respectively, while the material thickness of the inner tube is 0.1 cm made of Aluminum. The counter flow type was used for heat exchange, where the hot nanofluid was flowing inside the inner tube at 60 °C while the cold fluid (pure water) was flowing inside the outer tube at 25 °C. Three different types of nanofluids were used, namely (a) Copper oxide CuO (b) Aluminum oxide Al2O3 and (c) Titanium oxide TiO2. 0.05% and 0.2% concentrations based on the volume were tested in this investigation. The thermal properties of nanofluid, such as density, specific heat, and thermal conductivity, were calculated using Eqs. (1-3), and the values are shown in Table 1 .

The density of nanofluid can be calculated as:
(1)

where ρp, ρbf are the densities of nanoparticles and base fluid, respectively, and φ is the nanoparticle volume fraction.

(2)

where Cpp and Cpbf are the specific heat of particle and base fluid, respectively. Thermal conductivity for nanofluids was calculated based on the Maxwell model as follows:
(3)

where λp and λf are the thermal conductivities of particle and base fluid, respectively.

Three-dimensional numerical simulations were carried out using Ansys Fluent 18.2 to solve the governing equations for counter-flow heat exchanger, both flow and temperature fields, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a). The finite volume method (FVM) was used to discretize the governing equations with appropriate boundary conditions. The upwind scheme was used to calculate the convective terms at the control volume cell faces, while the SIMPLE algorithm was used to link the pressure and velocity fields (pressure-velocity coupling) [24].

The governing equations in vector form for mass, momentum, and energy can be written as follows:

Where Gk represents the generation of turbulence kinetic energy due to the mean velocity gradients in equations (7) and (8) as in Eq. (9).

C1ε, and C2ε are constants in k and ε. σk and σε are the turbulent Prandtl numbers for k and ε equations, respectively. The values ofC1ε , C2ε, σk and σε were 1.44, 1.92, 1, and 1.3 respectively. Additional info about the k epsilon turbulence model is in reference [25].

The turbulent viscosity is calculated by combining k and ε as in Eq. (10).
(10)

where C μ was giving a value of 0.09. The friction factor was calculated for the surface of inner tube as shown in Eq. (11).
(11)

the average shear stress on the inner wall of the inner tube and

the bulk velocity inside the inner tube.

Pref, and ρref) the fluid's pressure and density at the reference condition. The friction factor for distilled water was used to normalize the friction factor for the nanofluid inside the inner tube. For heat transfer calculations, the logarithmic mean temperature difference (LMTD) method was used to calculate the heat transfer coefficient for nanofluid as described in the following equations.
(12)
LMTD =

1
R thermal

Where Qh, Ui, Ai and Rthermal are the heat transfer rate, overall heat transfer coefficient, the surface heat transfer area for the inner tube, and total thermal resistance of the heat exchanger, respectively. Then the heat transfer coefficient of nanofluid is calculated from the overall heat transfer coefficient of the inner pipe after finding the conduction thermal resistance and convection thermal resistance for the outer tube by using Eqs. (15) and (16), respectively.
(15)

(16)

A o
where Di and Do are the inner and the outer diameter of the inner tube, respectively, k is the thermal conductivity of internal tube material, L is the length of the heat exchanger, ho is the heat transfer coefficient at the outer side of the inner tube (inner side of the outer tube).

In order to find ℎo, the average Nusselt number for the outer tube is calculated using the following correlations as follows [26]:
(17)

number

Where Pr is the Prandtl number for pure water, and DH is hydraulic diameter which is equal to the difference between outer and inner diameters. The heat transfer coefficient at the outer side of the inner tube can be obtained as
(21)
h O
=

The average Nusselt number is expressed as
(22)

Where

is the average temperature on the inner wall of the inner tube and

A
udA
is the bulk temperature inside the inner tube.

The Nusselt number for distilled water was used to normalize the Nusselt number for the nanofluid inside the inner tube. The thermo-hydraulic performance index was calculated using the following equation.
(23)
PI =
N
u nf
/
N
u bf
(
f nf
/
f bf
)
1 / 3

The type of mesh used in this study combined tetrahedron and sweep methods. The sweep type has a low count due to the high aspect ratio and offers perfect alignment with the flow field during the numerical simulation. Moreover, the boundary layer of the flow field was resolved by using the inflation layers adjacent to the wall of the inner and outer tubes, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Grid independence is one of the most essential things for the numerical simulation of fluid and heat cases. Three different numbers of grids with three different numbers of elements were used for the grid independence study. These elements were 377,281, 522,567, and 909,708. The number of elements 522,567 was selected after considering the changing of the results, such as temperature, friction coefficient, and Nusselt number are less than one percentage. The boundary conditions used in the simulation are shown in Fig. 2(c).

The two dimensional distribution or contour plot of temperature for the three types of Nano fluids is shown in Fig. 3 . The counter flow configuration is shown clearly in the figure where the cold flow enter from the left while the hot flow enter from the right. The range of the temperature was varying from 26.04 °C to 61.69 °C as depict in the legend of Fig. 3.

The effect of using different nanofluids on the average Nusselt number and pressure drop were investigated both experimentally and numerically for different volume fractions and Reynolds numbers. Fig. 4 compares the average Nusselt number between experimental and numerical results for different nanofluids (CuO, Al2O3, and TiO3 using water as a base fluid) and volume fractions of 0.05% and 0.2%, respectively. Fig. s 4(a) and 4(b) illustrate an excellent agreement between experimental and numerical results for both volumes of fractions (0.05% and 0.2%) at various Reynolds numbers. Fig. 4 shows that the average Nusselt number increases significantly with an increase in the Reynolds number. Furthermore, Fig. 4 demonstrates that as the volume fraction increases, the average Nusselt number increases for all values of the Reynolds number. Fig. 4 shows an interesting observation that CuO-water nanofluid exhibits the highest average Nusselt number compared with other nanofluids. This enhancement is more profound at Reynolds numbers below 9000. As the Reynolds number increases above 9000, using different nanofluids has a small effect on the average Nusselt number values.

The comparisons between the current study and some numerical and experimental work obtained by [27,28] is shown in Fig. 5 . The results for this study showed good agreement with the previous mentioned work.

Fig. 6 shows the ratio of the average Nusselt number of nanofluids to pure water using different nanofluids at various Reynolds numbers and volume fractions. Fig. 6 confirms that nanofluids enhance heat transfer characteristics compared with pure water. Moreover, this enhancement is more profound at Reynolds number of 2000. As the Reynolds number increases, the ratio of nanofluid average Nusselt number to pure water decreases until significantly at the studied Reynolds number of 12,000 and volume fractions of 0.05% and 0.2%. This indicates that larger volume fractions should be used at high Reynolds numbers. Once again, Fig. 6 demonstrates that CuO-water nanofluid exhibited the largest ratio of the average Nusselt number compared with other nanofluids. This effect is insignificant at a large Reynold number (Re = 12,000), as depicted in Fig. 6.

Fig. 7 presents the numerical results for the nanofluid's overall heat transfer coefficient ratio to pure water using different nanofluids at various Reynolds numbers and volume fractions. The results show that this ratio is always larger than one, which supports the results obtained in Fig. 6. Once again, as the Reynolds number increases, this ratio decreases. The average Nusselt number depends on the Reynolds number and Prandtl number according to Eqs. (18) and (19). Based on the thermal properties of various nanofluids listed in Table 1, CuO-water nanofluid shows the largest Prandtl number compared with other nanofluids. Consequently, it may exhibit the largest Nusselt number for the fixed Reynolds number.

Fig. 8 (a) compares the pressure drop between numerical and experimental results for different nanofluids using various Reynolds numbers and volume fractions. An excellent agreement was found between both results. Fig. 8(a) shows that the pressure drop increases with an increase in Reynolds number. Moreover, Fig. 8(a) shows that CuO–water nanofluid exhibits the largest pressure drop compared with other nanofluids. This can be attributed to the CuO-water nanofluid having the largest effective viscosity, as shown in Table 1. The effect of varying the volume fraction on the pressure drop for different nanofluids is depicted in Fig. 8(a). It is interesting to note that the pressure drop of CuO-water nanofluid is significantly affected by varying the volume fraction. Fig. 8(b) shows the variation of the friction factor with Reynolds number and volume fraction for different nanofluids. The trend of the friction factor for all nanofluids is decreasing with increasing Reynolds.

Further, the results show that the friction factor of Al2O3 nanofluid has the lowest value compared with other nanofluids. Fig. 9 illustrates the effect of varying Reynolds number and volume fraction on nanofluid's average friction factor ratio to pure water. The results show that the average friction factor ratio of all nanofluids for the studied range of Reynolds number is almost constant.

Moreover, Fig. 9 shows that CuO-water nanofluid exhibits the largest ratio of the average friction factor. Also, Fig. 9 illustrates that as the volume fraction of nanofluid increases, the ratio of the average friction factor increases for a fixed value of Reynolds number. This can be attributed to the increase in the viscosity.
Finally, the effect of varying Reynolds number and volume fraction of nanofluids on the thermo-hydraulic performance index inside the inner tube is depicted in Fig. 10 .

The results show that the performance index decreases for all nanofluids as the Re number increases. This decreasing trend is expected because the heat transfer enhancement ratio presented in Fig. 5 is decreasing, and the friction factor ratio is almost constant for the studied range of Reynolds numbers.

All nanofluids show a higher heat transfer coefficient than the base fluid. The enhancement heat transfer ratio for all nanofluid decrease as the Reynolds number increases from 2000 to 12,000. The friction factor ratio stays the same for the studied range of Reynolds numbers for all types of nanofluids. The CuO nanofluids for both volume concentrations, 0.05%, and 0.2%, have the highest performance index among the others type of nanofluids. Thermal physical properties play an important role in the performance of nanofluids according to the bulk motion of nanoparticles and Brownian motion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Upgrading the performance of shell and helically coiled heat exchangers with new flow path by using TiO2/water and CuO–TiO2/water nanofluids
Int. J. Therm. Sci.
